Simmons licenses, his brand Disney licenses, their brand, it can be a leverageable asset whether it's a corporate brand or a personal brand. Okay, even Zig Ziglar at one point got paid to be used in advertising to recruit franchisees for Valpak. And Zig is a celebrity, he's in our category, he's a famous person nobody's ever heard of. I mean he's famous but only to certain people. So your personal brand or your business brand and the reputation attached to it can be a leverageable asset, which means incidentally you want to think about how you build it and you want to think about how you protect it. The worst thing you can ever do in your reaching for more income is make compromises about what you sell or endorse to your customers because you're creating income at the expense of an asset, not in a way that builds an asset. And the corollary to that is getting in bed with people who can make you a lot of money but long term are cancerous and destructive. And so we all need to be very careful of that. So again, so John described his joint venture or partnership or working relationship with this superstar of the fitness world, John's a smart guy, I'm sure he was very careful. Because that guy could have shown up on the door with five different ways that we make a million dollars by joining forces tomorrow but the million dollars could come at huge, huge, huge expense. You don't see how careful we are about that because of course you don't see everything we choose not to do. You only see what we choose to do. And we make mistakes but not many. But if you saw everything we didn't do and everybody we politely declined to be involved with, the list is long and it's an endless constant process and in many cases Bill and I forego a lot of money in favor of in one way, shape or form there's going to be some back flush off of this that really didn't go be pretty. And better we protect the asset. Territory has an asset. So territory, I don't necessarily mean geography. In corporate language they would talk about market share, market space, some form of distribution you have. So in the candy aisle at Walmart there's only so many spaces. There's a finite number of territories for products to be sold on QVC. There's a finite amount of time. So being able to secure some and hold on to it is a very, very leverageable asset in a world like QVC or HSN. So if you think about are we to the point can I mention super conference speakers or you prefer not? Yes? Okay. So if you pay attention to QVC at all, you know that there's really a handful of product categories that work really, really well on QVC because really it's a Tupperware party, it's a home party with 50,000 people attending. And there's some product categories that it really takes a unique set of circumstances. So like Tony works on QVC, no other personal growth, personal development, nothing else that's ever been tried has ever worked on QVC. It's Tony. And there's only one Tony. But generally as a category it's no good. But cosmetic skincare, jewelry and food for example work really, really well on QVC.
